perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who have received material, nonpublic information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who have received material, nonpublic information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person only when there is guaranteed intent to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text refers to reasonable foreseeability related to securities trading which aligns with insider trading regulations. The modified text changes the 'reasonably foreseeable' standard to 'guaranteed intent.' This creates a much higher burden of proof, misaligning it with the actual legal standard for insider trading.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact, or omitting a material fact necessary to make the statements made not misleading, or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified contract language, by requiring 'guaranteed intent,' would make it harder to prove a violation of Rule 10b-5 because the SEC does not need to prove guaranteed intent, but rather that the person was in possession of material non-public information when making a trade.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'reasonably foreseeable' with 'guaranteed intent' regarding the communication of material, nonpublic information. This contradicts Rule 10b-5, which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states that it is unlawful to 'employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modification weakens the standard for insider trading liability by requiring proof of 'guaranteed intent,' whereas the law focuses on preventing actions that 'operate as a fraud or deceit,' regardless of intent. This creates a contradiction because proving 'guaranteed intent' is a much higher burden than proving an action 'operates as a fraud.'

perturbation.original_text: In case any provision of this Agreement shall be invalid, illegal or unenforceable, the validity, legality and enforceability of the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall not in any way be affected or impaired thereby.
perturbation.changed_text: In case any provision of this Agreement shall be invalid, illegal or unenforceable, the validity, legality and enforceability of the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall be determined at the sole discretion of the Genzme Corporation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a standard severability clause. The modified text allows Genzme Corporation to decide if the remaining provisions are enforceable, contradicting standard contract law principles regarding severability and potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law deals with the enforceability of agreements. The modification introduces an element of arbitrariness, where the enforceability of the contract depends on one party's discretion rather than legal principles, potentially conflicting with the requirement of mutual assent and consideration for a valid contract, and the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modification allows Genzme Corporation sole discretion to determine the enforceability of remaining provisions if any part of the agreement is deemed invalid. This contradicts the fundamental principles of contract law, where enforceability is typically determined by legal standards and mutual agreement, not the sole discretion of one party. While the provided law snippet from NYSenate.gov primarily concerns agreements required to be in writing, the Law Explanation highlights the modification's potential conflict with mutual assent, consideration, and the implied covenant of good faith. Giving one party unilateral power to decide enforceability introduces arbitrariness, making the contract potentially unenforceable due to lack of mutual agreement on essential terms if a provision is deemed invalid, illegal, or unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by an construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such State.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by an construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, except in cases of dispute where the laws of Massachusetts shall apply.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies New York law as the governing law. The modified text introduces a conflict by stating Delaware law governs, except when there's a dispute, then Massachusetts law applies. This creates ambiguity and potential conflict of laws issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws addresses choice of law clauses. While parties can generally choose the governing law, the choice must have a reasonable relationship to the contract and not violate fundamental public policy. The modification introduces uncertainty and makes the choice of law arbitrary. The Massachusetts exception is not related to the contract or the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. [Last reviewed in August of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS multi-jurisdictional law conflicts of law THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure legal practice/ethics wex articles wex definitions civil procedure constitutional law criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Resources menu of sources Federal Material U.S. Constitution Article VI - Supremacy Clause ; CRS Annotated text Article IV, Section 1 - Full Faith and Credit Clause ; CRS Annotated text Federal Statutes 28 U.S.C. §§1738-1739 - State Proceedings 18 U.S.C. §§ 848 , 896 , 927 , 2345 47 U.S.C. § 741- Satellite and Communications Law 19 U.S.C. § 2504 - Trade Agreements 45 U.S.C. § 1213 - Railroads Federal Judicial Decisions U.S. Supreme Court: Recent Conflicts Decisions liibulletin Oral Argument Previews U.S. Circuit Courts of Appeals: Recent Conflicts Decisions State Material State Judicial Decisions N.Y. Scoles and Peter Hay, Hornbook on Conflict of Laws , West Group (2004) other topics Category: International, Transnational, and Comparative Law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by introducing conflicting choices of law. The original text clearly states New York law governs. The changed text introduces Delaware law as the primary governing law but then adds an exception for disputes, where Massachusetts law applies. This directly contradicts the principle of a clear and consistent choice of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' The modified contract creates this exact scenario, making the governing law uncertain and potentially leading to disputes over which law applies. This ambiguity undermines the enforceability and predictability of the contract, fitting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement, neither party shall, without the prior written consent of the other party, directly or indirectly solicit the employment of any employee of such other party, unless such employee seeks employment on an unsolicited basis or in response to general solicitations or advertising or third party employment agencies, provided that such general solicitations or advertising is made, taken out and distributed in the ordinary course and not in an effort to specifically target employees of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement, neither party shall, without the verbal consent of the other party, directly solicit the employment of any employee of such other party, unless such employee seeks employment on an unsolicited basis.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause restricts solicitation of employees, requiring written consent. The modified clause changes 'written consent' to 'verbal consent', removes the exception for general solicitations, and removes indirect solicitations. Verbal agreements can be more difficult to prove than written ones, making enforcement uncertain and creating a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds (varying by state, generally requiring certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, to be in writing)
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-701 requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. While a no-solicitation clause itself may not always fall under the Statute of Frauds, removing the 'written' requirement complicates enforcement and could be problematic if the agreement's term extends beyond one year (as the agreement itself has a term of one year, extendable by the confidentiality provisions). The change to 'verbal consent' directly contradicts the purpose of the Statute of Frauds, which is to prevent fraudulent claims based on oral agreements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts NY GOL § 5-701, which requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By changing 'written consent' to 'verbal consent,' the modified contract weakens the enforceability of the no-solicitation clause, especially if the agreement's term extends beyond one year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes NY GOL § 5-701, stating that agreements not to be performed within one year must be in writing. The original contract requires written consent which provides a clear record, whereas verbal consent is difficult to prove and enforce, potentially contradicting the statute of frauds as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which highlights the purpose of requiring certain contracts to be in writing to prevent fraud.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and agrees that no contract or agreement providing for any Transaction involving the parties shall be deemed to exist between the parties unless and until a final definitive agreement has been executed and delivered by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and agrees that a contract or agreement providing for any Transaction involving the parties can be established through email exchanges if both parties express interest.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that only a final definitive agreement creates a contract. The modified text suggests email exchanges can form a contract, contradicting the need for a 'final definitive agreement.' This creates legal uncertainty about when a binding agreement exists.
perturbation.law_explanation: UETA § 7(a) states that a record or signature may not be denied legal effect or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form. However, this section does not change contract law with respect to what constitutes an offer and acceptance. The modified contract language implies that mere 'expression of interest' via email is enough to form a contract. This lowers the threshold of intent to contract and implies a contract can be formed without definitive terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Archives & Special Collections • Library • Penn Carey Law Skip to main content Home Library » Archives & Special Collections Archives & Special Collections The Archives and Special Collections Department of Biddle Law Library preserves, promotes, and provides access to the papers and records of two major legal organizations: The American Law Institute (ALI), and the National Bankruptcy Archives. The Department also cares for a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and archives from the University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School. Search Center Skip Sidebar Navigation Library Expand mobile section navigation Research Using The Library Faculty Services Archives & Special Collections About the Library News Library Map Library Hours Contact Us Collections The Archives of the American Law Institute (ALI) The ALI was established in 1923 by judges, lawyers, and law professors in an effort to promote the clarification, simplification, and scholarship of the law. The ALI Archives contains over 800 cubic feet of material documenting the administration, organization and work of the Institute, from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase,
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a 'final definitive agreement' for a transaction to exist. The changed text allows email exchanges expressing interest to form a contract, lowering the bar for contract formation. The Law Explanation mentions UETA § 7(a), stating that electronic form alone does not negate legal effect, but clarifies it doesn't alter contract law's requirements for offer and acceptance. The change contradicts the original text's explicit requirement for a 'final definitive agreement', creating uncertainty and potentially circumventing the need for such an agreement. The scraped snippets do not directly address contract formation, but the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly highlight the contradiction introduced by the altered text. This contradiction aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' as it deviates from standard contractual requirements.
